exemplar.title: To what extent does the mass tourism in the Charminar hotspot impact land use change and environmental quality in the neighborhoods?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the mass tourism in the Charminar hotspot impact land use change and environmental quality in the neighborhoods?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear, concise geographical research question with explicit theoretical links allowing hypothesis formation
Precise study area description with coordinates and rationale for primary data collection
Strong curricular linkage to IBDP themes and models, demonstrating deep contextual understanding
weaknesses: Mapping conventions incomplete (no legend for sampling sites, inconsistent labelling)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student convincingly meets the top two strands with a fully geographical question linked to theory and a precise, well-justified location. Although the map lacks one or two conventions, this is a minor omission and does not detract from the overall clarity and feasibility of the fieldwork design. Therefore, the work merits the maximum 3 marks for Criterion A.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods described (ranial transects, sensors, quadrat counts) are clearly linked to the research question
Effective visualization of planning and action plan demonstrating organizational clarity
weaknesses: Justification for device selection and sampling intervals is brief
Limited temporal and spatial representativeness (weekday only, short transect) affects reliability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Techniques are described with clear relevance and implemented correctly, but the rationale for choices and reliability considerations are only partially addressed. This sits squarely in the band for 2 marks.

strengths: All data collected are directly relevant and sufficient to support basic statistical testing
Use of multiple presentation formats (tables, bar and scatter graphs) appropriate to the data
General adherence to conventions (titles, axes, units) across most tables and figures
weaknesses: Occasional errors in conventions (mis-labelled chi-square table, inconsistent decimals)
Lack of source citations for background statistics and chi-square distribution table
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The information treatment meets the 3â4 band robustly: data are relevant and sufficiently presented with mostly correct conventions. Minor formatting and citation errors prevent the highest band but justify a mid-band score of 4.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive statistics and chi-square testing linked to hypotheses
Identification and explanation of trends and anomalies with reference to spatial theory
Integration of photographic evidence to contextualize anomalous data points
weaknesses: Statistical significance and confidence levels are not fully reported or interpreted
Gaps in linking some environmental variables (sound, Oâ) back to theoretical frameworks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates solid use of statistical and descriptive techniques combined with clear pattern linking to theory, consistent with the 5â6 band. However, incomplete reporting of significance levels and some explanatory gaps preclude the top marks within this band, warranting 5 out of 8.

strengths: Conclusion clearly synthesizes findings for both hypotheses and is directly supported by data trends and statistical results
weaknesses: Some emissions figures in the conclusion lack explicit linkage back to the research question and hypotheses
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear, concise, and fully supported by the analysis, satisfying the descriptor for 2 marks in Criterion E. A minor coherence gap does not undermine the overall quality.

strengths: Evaluation outlines relevant methodological strengths and weaknesses impacting validity
Improvement suggestions (extended transect, weekend sampling, additional variables) are logical and pertinent
weaknesses: Discussion of the impact of each suggested improvement on data quality is brief
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation meets the requirements for band 2 by appropriately outlining strengths, weaknesses, and relevant improvements. The lack of detailed impact explanation prevents reaching the highest band, so 2 marks are awarded.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two relevant maps are included (national, district and Google-Earth close-up) with north arrow and scale visible, showing where data points lie.  However, some cartographic conventions are missing (no clear legend for sampling sites, inconsistent labelling) so the work fits the âmap included but with excess detail / lacking conventionsâ description.

The student formulates a clear, concise and fully geographical research question that specifically addresses spatial patterns around the Charminar hotspot.  Explicit theoretical links are provided to Butlerâs TALC, Hawkinsâ model, distance-decay and central-place theory, which together justify the formation of two testable hypotheses.  This satisfies the descriptor of a question that is geographical with clear theoretical links allowing for hypothesis formation.

The IA contains tables, bar graph, four scatter graphs with trend lines and two chi-square worked examples.  These are appropriate for the data type but not necessarily the *most* effective (e.g., box plots for dispersion, GIS proportional symbols).  Hence the middle band (3-4) is appropriate.

Data collection techniques were implemented correctly (replicate readings, 15 sites, use of Strava for distance).  However, reliability remains moderate: weekday-only, monsoon conditions and short 650 m transect limit representativeness, so the techniques cannot be judged âhigh-qualityâ.

The student employs descriptive statistics, a simple chi-square test and adds best-fit lines, which are appropriate but elementary; interpretation of significance levels is partly misconstrued.  This fits âappropriate descriptive and statistical techniquesâ (3-4 marks) but not the higher band requiring confidence levels.

Trends and anomalies are identified and repeatedly linked back to distance-decay, bid-rent and central-place theory, demonstrating clear linkage to the research question.  Yet not every variable (sound, Oâ) is fully reconciled with theory, leaving some explanatory gaps, hence 3-4 band.

A precise study area (17.36Â° N, 78.47Â° E) is stated and supported by narrative description of surrounding land uses and tourist flows.  The text explains why 0â500 m transects are feasible for primary data collection and how four radial roads provide access.  This level of locational specificity fully fulfils the highest band.

The evaluation names several strengths (multiple sensors, averaging of four roads) and weaknesses (seasonality, weekday bias, short transect) and briefly explains their effect on validity, which aligns with âappropriate outline of strengths/weaknessesâ (band 2).

Most variables measured (shops, COâ, Oâ, litter, sound) are directly relevant to the hypotheses and the dataset is sufficient to undertake basic statistical testing, though spatial extent is short and temporal coverage limited.  This aligns with âmost data relevant allowing partial analysisâ.

The conclusion restates findings for each hypothesis and backs them with reference to data trends and chi-square results, satisfying âclear conclusion fully supported by analysisâ.  This meets the top band for Criterion E (2 marks).

Both hypotheses are analysed and major patterns discussed, but some supporting evidence (e.g., statistical validation for sound/Oâ) is weak and spatial variability beyond 500 m is unexplored.  Thus analysis is âwith some gaps in evidenceâ (3-4 marks).

Methods such as radial transect sampling every 50 m, quadrat litter counts, COâ/Oâ sensors and sound meters are explained and linked to each hypothesis.  Nevertheless, justification of why these particular devices and interval lengths were chosen is brief, and sampling strategy (e.g. day-of-week bias) is only partially considered, placing the work in the âdescribed with clear relevanceâ band rather than âcomprehensively explainedâ.

Basic conventions (titles, axes, units) are generally present, yet there are several slips: mis-labelled chi-square table, inconsistent decimal places and occasional missing units.  These âoccasional errorsâ match the 3-4 band.

Improvement suggestions include extending transect to 3 km, sampling weekends vs weekdays, adding temperature data and adjusting interval lengthâall relevant and logically connected to limitations, yet the discussion of impact remains brief.  This fits ârelevant suggestions outlinedâ (band 2) rather than the more detailed highest band.

annotations.comment: Hypotheses are concise, spatially explicit, and clearly linked to the research question, providing a solid framework for testing.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Justification for Hypothesis 1 is descriptive but should explicitly reference distanceâdecay and bidârent theory to justify the expected spatial decline in shops.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly formulated, explicitly geographical, and linked to Butlerâs TALC and central-place theory, allowing for testable hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Background theory includes useful statistics but lacks citations for figures such as tourist growth and NOâ levels; incorporate proper references to strengthen credibility.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Syllabus linkage is well articulated, with explicit reference to the IBDP theme, optional theme E and page number, demonstrating curricular relevance.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Local relevance section describes issues but could be strengthened by specifying how landâuse changes and traffic patterns directly inform the sampling feasibility.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Table 3 headings are overcrowded and expected values for Oâ and sound are unclear; simplify layout and highlight key summary statistics.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Analysis for Hypothesis 1 links trends to distanceâdecay, bidârent and CPT effectively, showing integration of theory and data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Enhance Hypothesis 2 interpretation by reporting statistical significance for COâ and litter trends to strengthen analytical linkage.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Consider quantifying scatterplot correlations (e.g. RÂ² values) to support narrative claims of environmental variable trends.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Study area description successfully situates Charminar with precise coordinates, surrounding land uses and rationale for primary data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Consider adding a smallâscale locator inset to show the study areaâs position within greater Hyderabad for enhanced spatial context.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Map follows basic conventions but lacks a legend for sampling sites and transect points; add a clear legend to meet mapping standards.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The planning and action plan visualization effectively outlines preparatory steps and workflow, demonstrating methodological organization.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Field team division is described but lacks rationale for ensuring consistency across teams; clarify how interâteam variability was minimized.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Use of COâ/Oâ sensors and replication at 15 sites demonstrates appropriate technique aligned to the question, yielding usable primary data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Justify the choice of 50 m quadrat intervals in terms of representativeness and statistical power to strengthen methodological rationale.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Table 1 presents observed and expected values clearly and supports chiâsquare calculation; presentation is appropriate for hypothesis 1.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Chiâsquare distribution table lacks source citation and has inconsistent decimal formatting; ensure full adherence to conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The chiâsquare calculation is accurately displayed using LaTeX formatting, demonstrating clear mathematical treatment.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Interpret the calculated ÏÂ² value against the specific critical value and pâvalue, explaining the significance threshold in the text.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Table 3 compiles multiâvariable means for Hypothesis 2 effectively, facilitating comparative analysis across COâ, Oâ, sound, and litter.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 2 anomaly at the 200â250 m junction is well explained using field photograph, adding valuable spatial insight.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: PM emission figures are interesting but need clear linkage back to the fieldwork question and hypotheses for coherence.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: Conclusion for Hypothesis 1 succinctly synthesizes findings with theory and statistics, fully supported by the analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: Evaluation clearly outlines methodological strengths, including multiple sensors and averaging across roads, demonstrating appropriate reflection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Improvement suggestions are logical but would benefit from explaining the impact of each change on data validity and representativeness.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Traffic period graph is informative but should be explicitly connected to the chosen fieldwork time window to justify sampling period.
